content.text: Building a business case for an agentic AI program requires more than a single ROI number. Token consumption is metered usage — it scales with volume, changes with model selection, and compounds across every workflow the agent touches. Rigorous scenario planning forces teams to stress-test the economics across conservative, moderate, and optimistic assumptions before committing capital.

attrs.alt: Three-scenario comparison table showing conservative, moderate, and optimistic cost-benefit outcomes for an agentic AI program including token cost, FTE saved, annual benefit, TCO, net benefit, payback period, 3-year ROI, and decision recommendation

content.text: What It Is

Cost-benefit scenario planning for agentic AI is a structured financial modeling process that evaluates a proposed agent program under at least three distinct assumption sets — conservative, moderate, and optimistic — and computes key economic metrics (total cost of ownership, net benefit, payback period, and ROI) for each. Token economics sits at the center of the cost side: unlike traditional software licensing with predictable annual fees, LLM inference is priced per million tokens consumed, which varies with prompt length, retrieval context, chain-of-thought depth, and call frequency.

A scenario plan is not a forecast — it is a range of credible futures. The conservative scenario uses high-cost assumptions (expensive models, low automation rates, high human-in-the-loop overhead) to test whether the program can survive worst-case economics. The optimistic scenario stress-tests the upside to ensure projections do not become the target that sponsors anchor to. The moderate scenario becomes the planning baseline for budgeting, headcount, and milestone targets.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI investments fail the business case review for two common reasons. First, teams present a single-point ROI estimate with no range, so the first cost overrun destroys stakeholder confidence. Second, teams underestimate token costs because they benchmark against simple chatbot usage rather than the substantially longer prompts and multi-step reasoning chains that autonomous agents generate. A customer-service agent handling a single ticket might generate 5,000–15,000 tokens across its reasoning trace, tool calls, and response — versus a few hundred tokens for a simple Q&A interaction.

Scenario planning solves both problems. It forces explicit documentation of every cost and benefit assumption, surfaces the conditions under which the program breaks even, and gives executives a transparent view of what must be true for each outcome. When actual results land in the conservative bucket, the team has a pre-approved response path (scope reduction, model downgrade, additional automation) rather than an impromptu defense of a missed number.
Benefits
Executive alignment:
Showing three outcomes instead of one signals analytical rigor and invites sponsors to choose their risk tolerance rather than accept a single number.
Budget defensibility:
The conservative scenario sets the ceiling for budget approval; the moderate scenario drives headcount and tooling spend plans.
Token cost visibility:
Forces the team to measure actual prompt/completion token counts during prototyping before committing to cost projections.
Break-even clarity:
Computing payback period for each scenario reveals how sensitive the investment is to adoption speed and automation rate.
Contingency planning:
Each scenario can be paired with a pre-defined management response — scope reduction, model swap, human-in-the-loop adjustment — so no scenario outcome is a surprise.
Trade-offs
Modeling effort:
A credible three-scenario model requires 2–4 weeks of measurement (token counts, handle times, error rates) before projections are defensible.
Parameter uncertainty:
Token prices, model capabilities, and labor costs all change faster than traditional software, making 3-year projections inherently speculative.
Anchoring risk:
Executives often mentally anchor to the optimistic scenario; scenario plans should explicitly label each scenario probability weight to counter this.
Indirect benefit difficulty:
Quality improvements, error rate reductions, and employee satisfaction gains are hard to convert to dollar amounts without historical benchmarks.
How It Works: Token Economics
Token-based pricing means that inference cost is a function of tokens consumed per call and call volume. For a given workflow, the monthly token cost can be expressed as:
Where
is daily call volume,
is average input tokens per call,
is average output tokens per call, and

are per-million-token prices. A multi-step agentic workflow compounds this: each tool call in the chain triggers an additional LLM call, multiplying token consumption by the average number of steps per task.

The three scenarios primarily differ on four levers: model tier (frontier vs. mid-tier vs. smaller specialized), average tokens per call (determined by context window usage and prompt engineering efficiency), automation rate (fraction of tasks the agent handles end-to-end without human escalation), and daily call volume (adoption curve). Conservative scenarios use frontier model pricing with low automation rates; optimistic scenarios assume optimized prompts, caching, and higher automation after ramp.
Architecture: Scenario Planning Process

The scenario planning process flows through five stages: measurement, assumption setting, model construction, sensitivity analysis, and decision framing. In the measurement stage, a working prototype is instrumented to capture real token counts per workflow step. These empirical measurements anchor the moderate scenario; the conservative scenario inflates token counts by 50–100% and reduces automation rate by 30%; the optimistic scenario uses caching and prompt compression to reduce token counts by 20–30% and assumes faster adoption.
When to Use It

Cost-benefit scenario planning is appropriate at the end of a discovery phase when a pilot or prototype has produced sufficient measurement data. It is premature during early ideation when token counts are speculative — scenario plans built on pure estimates (rather than measured values) can give false confidence. The technique is also valuable at major funding gates: when moving from pilot to production deployment, or when scaling an agent program from one business unit to enterprise-wide.

Scenario planning is less useful when the investment is small enough that business case formality is not required (internal tooling under $50K), or when the agent's primary value is qualitative (knowledge management, ideation support) with no meaningful cost-reduction numerator.
How to Implement
Step 1: Measure Token Consumption in the Prototype
Instrument the prototype to log input and output token counts for every LLM call. Aggregate across all steps of the workflow to produce a per-task token total.

import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

@dataclass
class TokenLedger:
    """Accumulates token usage across an agentic workflow run."""
    workflow_id: str
    input_tokens: int = 0
    output_tokens: int = 0
    steps: List[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def total_tokens(self) -> int:
        return self.input_tokens + self.output_tokens

    def record(self, step_name: str, usage: anthropic.types.Usage) -> None:
        self.input_tokens += usage.input_tokens
        self.output_tokens += usage.output_tokens
        self.steps.append({
            "step": step_name,
            "input": usage.input_tokens,
            "output": usage.output_tokens,
        })

    def cost_usd(
        self,
        price_per_million_input: float,
        price_per_million_output: float,
    ) -> float:
        return (
            self.input_tokens * price_per_million_input / 1_000_000
            + self.output_tokens * price_per_million_output / 1_000_000
        )


client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_instrumented_workflow(task: dict) -> TokenLedger:
    ledger = TokenLedger(workflow_id=task["id"])

    # Step 1: classify intent
    r1 = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Classify intent: {task['description']}"}],
    )
    ledger.record("classify_intent", r1.usage)

    # Step 2: generate action plan
    r2 = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[
            {"role": "user", "content": f"Task: {task['description']}\nIntent: {r1.content[0].text}\nCreate an action plan."},
        ],
    )
    ledger.record("generate_plan", r2.usage)

    return ledger

# Run across a sample of 50 real tasks to get empirical averages
results = [run_instrumented_workflow(t) for t in sample_tasks]
avg_input = sum(r.input_tokens for r in results) / len(results)
avg_output = sum(r.output_tokens for r in results) / len(results)
print(f"Average input tokens/task: {avg_input:.0f}")
print(f"Average output tokens/task: {avg_output:.0f}")
print(f"Average cost/task (Sonnet): ${results[0].cost_usd(3.0, 15.0):.4f}")
Step 2: Build the Scenario Model
With empirical token averages, build a spreadsheet or Python model that computes monthly and annual costs and benefits across the three scenarios.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ScenarioInputs:
    label: str
    # Token economics
    avg_input_tokens: float       # per task
    avg_output_tokens: float      # per task
    price_per_m_input: float      # USD per 1M input tokens
    price_per_m_output: float     # USD per 1M output tokens
    # Volume
    daily_tasks: int              # tasks handled per day
    automation_rate: float        # 0-1: fraction fully automated (no human review)
    # Benefits
    fte_hourly_cost: float        # loaded cost per hour
    minutes_saved_per_task: float # compared to manual process
    # One-time costs
    build_cost: float
    # Recurring non-token costs (annual): infra, tooling, maintenance FTE
    annual_overhead: float

def compute_scenario(s: ScenarioInputs) -> dict:
    # Monthly token cost
    monthly_tasks = s.daily_tasks * 30
    token_cost_per_task = (
        s.avg_input_tokens * s.price_per_m_input / 1_000_000
        + s.avg_output_tokens * s.price_per_m_output / 1_000_000
    )
    monthly_token_cost = monthly_tasks * token_cost_per_task
    annual_token_cost = monthly_token_cost * 12

    # Annual benefit: only automated tasks save the full time
    annual_hours_saved = (
        monthly_tasks * 12
        * s.automation_rate
        * s.minutes_saved_per_task / 60
    )
    annual_labor_benefit = annual_hours_saved * s.fte_hourly_cost

    # Total cost of ownership (Year 1)
    annual_tco = s.build_cost + annual_token_cost + s.annual_overhead

    net_benefit_yr1 = annual_labor_benefit - annual_tco

    # 3-year NPV (simplified; no discount rate for clarity)
    cost_yr2_3 = (annual_token_cost + s.annual_overhead) * 2
    benefit_yr2_3 = annual_labor_benefit * 2
    net_3yr = net_benefit_yr1 + benefit_yr2_3 - cost_yr2_3

    roi_3yr = net_3yr / (s.build_cost + (annual_token_cost + s.annual_overhead) * 3)

    # Payback period (months)
    monthly_net = (annual_labor_benefit - annual_token_cost - s.annual_overhead) / 12
    payback_months = s.build_cost / monthly_net if monthly_net > 0 else float("inf")

    return {
        "label": s.label,
        "monthly_token_cost": round(monthly_token_cost),
        "annual_tco": round(annual_tco),
        "annual_benefit": round(annual_labor_benefit),
        "net_benefit_yr1": round(net_benefit_yr1),
        "payback_months": round(payback_months, 1),
        "roi_3yr_pct": round(roi_3yr * 100, 1),
    }

# Empirical baseline from measurement: ~4,200 input, ~800 output tokens/task
conservative = ScenarioInputs(
    label="Conservative",
    avg_input_tokens=6_300, avg_output_tokens=1_200,   # +50% vs measured
    price_per_m_input=3.0, price_per_m_output=15.0,    # Sonnet pricing
    daily_tasks=400, automation_rate=0.45,
    fte_hourly_cost=60.0, minutes_saved_per_task=8,
    build_cost=180_000, annual_overhead=110_000,
)
moderate = ScenarioInputs(
    label="Moderate",
    avg_input_tokens=4_200, avg_output_tokens=800,
    price_per_m_input=3.0, price_per_m_output=15.0,
    daily_tasks=400, automation_rate=0.65,
    fte_hourly_cost=60.0, minutes_saved_per_task=8,
    build_cost=180_000, annual_overhead=80_000,
)
optimistic = ScenarioInputs(
    label="Optimistic",
    avg_input_tokens=3_000, avg_output_tokens=600,     # caching + prompt compression
    price_per_m_input=3.0, price_per_m_output=15.0,
    daily_tasks=400, automation_rate=0.82,
    fte_hourly_cost=60.0, minutes_saved_per_task=8,
    build_cost=180_000, annual_overhead=65_000,
)

for s in [conservative, moderate, optimistic]:
    result = compute_scenario(s)
    print(f"\n{result['label']}")
    print(f"  Monthly token cost: ${result['monthly_token_cost']:,}")
    print(f"  Annual TCO: ${result['annual_tco']:,}")
    print(f"  Annual benefit: ${result['annual_benefit']:,}")
    print(f"  Net benefit (Yr 1): ${result['net_benefit_yr1']:,}")
    print(f"  Payback: {result['payback_months']} months")
    print(f"  3-Year ROI: {result['roi_3yr_pct']}%")
Examples
Example 1: Simple Cost Estimator
Example 2: Model Tier Switching Analysis
A key optimization lever is routing simpler tasks to a cheaper model tier. The scenario model can compute cost savings from intelligent routing:

def routing_savings(
    daily_tasks: int,
    pct_routed_to_small: float,  # e.g. 0.60 = 60% go to haiku/mini
    avg_tokens: int,
    price_frontier: float,       # per M tokens, blended
    price_small: float,          # per M tokens, blended
) -> dict:
    monthly_tasks = daily_tasks * 30
    baseline = monthly_tasks * avg_tokens * price_frontier / 1_000_000
    with_routing = (
        monthly_tasks * pct_routed_to_small * avg_tokens * price_small / 1_000_000
        + monthly_tasks * (1 - pct_routed_to_small) * avg_tokens * price_frontier / 1_000_000
    )
    return {
        "baseline_monthly": round(baseline),
        "with_routing_monthly": round(with_routing),
        "monthly_savings": round(baseline - with_routing),
        "annual_savings": round((baseline - with_routing) * 12),
    }

print(routing_savings(
    daily_tasks=500, pct_routed_to_small=0.65,
    avg_tokens=4000, price_frontier=9.0, price_small=0.8,
))
# baseline_monthly: 54000, with_routing_monthly: 10440, annual_savings: 523920
Real-World Example

A financial services firm evaluated an agent for initial loan application review. Their prototype measured an average of 8,400 input tokens and 1,100 output tokens per application — driven largely by the retrieved regulatory context injected into each prompt. At 250 applications per day and Sonnet-tier pricing, the moderate scenario projected a monthly token cost of approximately $11,600. The firm's analyst team handled each application in 45 minutes; the agent reduced that to 12 minutes of human review (verification only), saving 33 minutes per application.

At a loaded analyst cost of $75/hour, the annual labor saving across a 70% automation rate was $5.4M — far exceeding the $480K annual TCO. The conservative scenario (40% automation, 50% higher token counts) still showed a 14-month payback and 180% 3-year ROI. This range gave the investment committee confidence to proceed even if adoption lagged.
Common Mistakes
Benchmarking against chatbot token usage:
A single-turn chatbot uses hundreds of tokens. A multi-step agent handling the same domain uses tens of thousands. Always measure from the actual agentic workflow, not from a simpler proxy.
Ignoring retrieval token overhead:
RAG-augmented agents inject retrieved documents into the context. A 3,000-token retrieved chunk doubles the input size of a 3,000-token base prompt. Include retrieval context in token measurements.
Omitting human-in-the-loop labor cost:
The agent does not save the full handle time for every task. Low-confidence or escalated tasks still require full human review. Model the automation rate conservatively to avoid overstating benefits.
Using a single-point estimate:
Presenting one ROI number invites challenges on every assumption. Three scenarios anchor the discussion on the range of outcomes rather than a single fragile number.
Forgetting multi-year token price trends:

Model prices have declined significantly year-over-year; a 3-year model that uses today's prices for all three years over-states costs in years 2 and 3. Build in a pricing trajectory assumption for each scenario.
Best Practices
Measure token counts from at least 30 representative real tasks before building any projection. Synthetic tasks systematically underestimate context length.
Separate one-time build costs from recurring operational costs. Investors use different discount rates for each category.
Model both token volume growth (adoption curve) and token efficiency improvements (prompt optimization, caching, model routing) as independent levers.
Assign a probability weight to each scenario (e.g., Conservative 25%, Moderate 55%, Optimistic 20%) and compute an expected-value ROI for the executive summary.
Refresh the model every quarter during the first year by replacing projected token counts with actuals, keeping the scenarios current as the program matures.
Document each assumption with its source (measured, estimated, industry benchmark) so reviewers can challenge inputs rather than conclusions.
Performance and Scalability Considerations

Token cost does not scale linearly with task volume because of caching effects. Prompt caching (available on major provider APIs) allows repeated system prompt content to be cached at a fraction of the full input price, often reducing effective input costs by 60–80% for agents with large, stable system prompts. This makes the cost-per-task curve sub-linear at scale — a critical insight that conservative scenarios should not ignore entirely.

At very high volumes (millions of tasks/month), enterprise discount tiers and reserved capacity pricing become available, further bending the cost curve downward. The scenario model should include a volume discount schedule that activates at defined thresholds in the optimistic scenario.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Provider dashboards (Anthropic Console, OpenAI Usage, Azure AI Studio) expose real-time token consumption by API key. For multi-model programs, tools like LangSmith, Helicone, and Portkey provide cross-provider token tracking with per-workflow attribution. These observability platforms also surface prompt vs. completion ratios and per-step token breakdowns — exactly the data needed to validate scenario model assumptions against production reality.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strength
Weakness
Best for
Three-scenario planning
Bounded range, executive clarity
Manual to maintain; three models to update
Funding gates, board presentations
Monte Carlo simulation
Full probability distribution
Requires parameter distributions, not just point estimates
Large investments (>$5M); risk quantification
Single-point ROI
Simple to communicate
Brittle; any variance destroys credibility
Small, low-risk internal tools
Security and Reliability

The scenario model itself carries a governance risk: if assumptions are not documented and version-controlled, the model becomes a source of confusion rather than clarity. Store the scenario model in a shared repository with change history. Lock the assumption inputs after the investment decision and track actuals against each scenario monthly. If actuals consistently land in the conservative scenario for two or more consecutive months, trigger a formal program review rather than waiting for a milestone.
Mental Model

Think of cost-benefit scenario planning like weather forecasting. A single temperature prediction for next week is almost certainly wrong. A forecast band ("high between 18°C and 24°C") is more useful because it sets appropriate expectations and lets people plan for the range. The three scenarios in a business case serve the same function: they replace a single fragile forecast with a credible range that gives decision-makers enough certainty to act while accurately conveying the uncertainty that remains.
Key Takeaways
Token economics is a variable, metered cost that scales with prompt length, step count, and call volume — measure it empirically from a working prototype before building projections.
Three scenarios (conservative, moderate, optimistic) make the business case more credible by replacing a single fragile forecast with a bounded range.
The automation rate is the highest-leverage assumption in the benefit calculation; conservative scenarios should use automation rates 30–40% below the prototype's peak performance.
Prompt caching and model routing can reduce effective token costs by 60–80% at scale; include these as optimistic-scenario levers, not baseline assumptions.
Refresh the model quarterly by replacing projected inputs with actuals; if two consecutive months land in the conservative scenario, trigger a formal program review.
Document every assumption with its source (measured vs. estimated) so reviewers can challenge inputs rather than disputing conclusions.
